title: शिव पञ्चाक्षर स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
ॐ नमः शिवाय शिवाय नमः ॐ
ॐ नमः शिवाय शिवाय नमः ॐ

नागेन्द्रहाराय त्रिलोचनाय
भस्माङ्गरागाय महेश्वराय।
नित्याय शुद्धाय दिगम्बराय
तस्मै नकाराय नमः शिवाय॥१॥

मन्दाकिनीसलिलचन्दनचर्चिताय
नन्दीश्वरप्रमथनाथमहेश्वराय।
मन्दारपुष्पबहुपुष्पसुपूजिताय
तस्मै मकाराय नमः शिवाय॥२॥

शिवाय गौरीवदनाब्जबृन्द-
सूर्याय दक्षाध्वरनाशकाय।
श्रीनीलकण्ठाय वृषध्वजाय
तस्मै शिकाराय नमः शिवाय॥३॥

वसिष्ठकुम्भोद्भवगौतमार्य-
मुनीन्द्रदेवार्चितशेखराय।
चन्द्रार्कवैश्वानरलोचनाय
तस्मै वकाराय नमः शिवाय॥४॥

यक्षस्वरूपाय जटाधराय
पिनाकहस्ताय सनातनाय।
दिव्याय देवाय दिगम्बराय
तस्मै यकाराय नमः शिवाय॥५॥

पञ्चाक्षरमिदं पुण्यं यः पठेच्छिवसन्निधौ।
शिवलोकमवाप्नोति शिवेन सह मोदते॥

॥ इति श्रीमच्छंकराचार्यविरचितं शिवपञ्चाक्षरस्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
Om Namah Shivaya Shivaya Namah Om
Om Namah Shivaya Shivaya Namah Om

Nagendraharaya Trilochanaya
Bhasmangaragaya Maheshvaraya.
Nityaya Shuddhaya Digambaraya
Tasmai Nakaraya Namah Shivaya.||1||

Mandakinisalilachandanacharchitaya
Nandishvarapramathanatha Maheshvaraya.
Mandarapushpabahupushpasupujitaya
Tasmai Makaraya Namah Shivaya.||2||

Shivaya Gaurivadanabjabrinda-
Suryaya Dakshadhvaranashakaya.
Shrinilakanthaya Vrishadhvajaya
Tasmai Shikaraya Namah Shivaya.||3||

Vasishtakumbhodbhavagautamarya-
Munindradevarchitashekaraya.
ChandrarkaVaishvanaralochanaya
Tasmai Vakaraya Namah Shivaya.||4||

Yakshasvaroopaya Jatadharaya
PinakaHastaya Sanatanaya.
Divyaya Devaya Digambaraya
Tasmai Yakaraya Namah Shivaya.||5||

Panchaksharamidam Punyam Yah Pathech-Chivasannidhau.
Shivalokam-Avapnoti Shivena Saha Modate.

|| Iti Shrimach-Chankaracharyavirachitam Shivapanchaksharastotram Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
न — नागों के राजा वासुकि का हार धारण करने वाले, तीन नेत्रों वाले, भस्म का अंगराग लगाने वाले, महेश्वर, नित्य, शुद्ध, दिगम्बर शिव को, उस 'न' कार को नमस्कार है।

म — मन्दाकिनी (गंगा) के जल और चन्दन से अर्चित, नन्दीश्वर और प्रमथगणों के नाथ महेश्वर, मन्दार और अनेक पुष्पों से पूजित शिव को, उस 'म' कार को नमस्कार है।

शि — कल्याणकारी, गौरी के मुखकमल के सूर्य समान, दक्ष के यज्ञ का नाश करने वाले, नीलकण्ठ, वृषभ-ध्वज शिव को, उस 'शि' कार को नमस्कार है।

वा — वसिष्ठ, अगस्त्य, गौतम आदि श्रेष्ठ मुनियों और देवताओं द्वारा अर्चित, चन्द्र, सूर्य और अग्नि तीन नेत्रों वाले शिव को, उस 'वा' कार को नमस्कार है।

य — यक्ष स्वरूप, जटाधारी, पिनाक हाथ में धारण करने वाले, सनातन, दिव्य, देव, दिगम्बर शिव को, उस 'य' कार को नमस्कार है।

जो व्यक्ति इस पवित्र पञ्चाक्षर स्तोत्र का शिव के सन्निधि में पाठ करता है, वह शिवलोक को प्राप्त करता है और शिव के साथ आनन्द को प्राप्त होता है।

viniyog.shloka: अस्य श्रीशिवपञ्चाक्षरस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य श्रीमच्छंकराचार्य ऋषिः। इन्द्रवज्रा छन्दः। श्रीसदाशिवो देवता। ॐ नमः शिवाय बीजम्। पञ्चाक्षरमन्त्रशक्तिः शक्तिः। शिवलोकप्राप्तिः कीलकम्। श्रीसदाशिवप्रीत्यर्थे पञ्चाक्षरस्तोत्रजपे विनियोगः॥

benefits: Explains the mystical significance of each syllable of Om Namah Shivaya
Invokes entry to shivaloka after death
Invokes the blessing of purity of mind and body
Powerful mantra-based stotra for daily recitation
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and negative karma
Traditionally associated with deep devotional connection with lord shiva
Recitation near a Shiva temple multiplies the merit
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Shiv Panchakshara Stotram (शिव पञ्चाक्षर स्तोत्रम्) stands as a devotional hymn of praise. Rooted in Composed by Adi Shankaracharya, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to explain the mystical significance of each syllable of Om Namah Shivaya and bring deep devotional connection with Lord Shiva. It is also said to bestow purity of mind and body. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan month for maximum spiritual benefit. At 5 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 4 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Shiv Panchakshara Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shiv Panchakshara Stotram (Nagendraharaya) in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Adi Shankaracharya's hymn on Om Namah Shivaya's five syllables.
alsoKnownAs: Na Ma Shi Va Ya Stotram
relatedSearches: Shiv Panchakshara Stotram lyrics Sanskrit Shankaracharya
Om Namah Shivaya five syllables meaning
Shiv Panchakshara English translation each verse
Difference Panchakshara stotram vs Panchakshara mantra

seoFaqs.question: What is the structural relationship between the stotram and the Panchakshara mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiv Panchakshara Stotram consists of five verses, each beginning with one of the five syllables of the Panchakshara mantra Namah Shivaya, namely Na, Ma, Shi, Va and Ya. The first verse opens with Nagendraharaya (the one wearing the king of serpents as garland), the second with Mandakini-salila (the one bathed by the Ganga's waters), the third with Shivaya gauri-vadana (Shiva who is the husband of Gauri), the fourth with Vasishtha-kumbhodbhava (worshipped by Vasishtha and Agastya), and the fifth with Yajnaswarupaya (the embodiment of yajna). This deliberate alphabetic ordering binds the stotram to the mantra so that each chanted verse is also a meditative unfolding of one syllable's meaning.

seoFaqs.question: What is the elemental correspondence of the five syllables?
seoFaqs.answer: Tantric Shaiva texts and the Wikipedia entry on Shiva Panchakshara Stotra state that each syllable corresponds to one of the five great elements (pancha-mahabhutas): Na to Prithvi (earth), Ma to Jala (water), Shi to Agni (fire), Va to Vayu (air) and Ya to Akasha (ether or space). When a sadhaka recites the stotram contemplatively, each verse is therefore not merely a praise of Shiva's iconographic form but an invocation of the corresponding cosmic element through Shiva. Some teachers extend this to chakra-correspondences (Muladhara, Svadhisthana, Manipura, Anahata, Vishuddha), making the recitation a layered tantric practice. This elemental layering distinguishes the Panchakshara recitation from a simple devotional hymn and explains its centrality in Shaiva sadhana.

seoFaqs.question: Who composed the Shiv Panchakshara Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The composition is attributed to Sri Adi Shankaracharya (8th century CE) and is preserved in the Shankara Granthavali editions of the Shringeri, Kanchi and Dwaraka mathas. Wikipedia and Shlokam.org both record the same attribution, and the verses display Shankara's signature combination of Vedantic vocabulary, Puranic iconography and bhakti rasa. A few regional manuscript traditions ascribe the hymn to later Shaiva acharyas, but the dominant tradition across South Indian temple recitation and North Indian Shaiva householder practice treats it as a Shankara composition. It is included as nitya-paath at Sringeri, Kalady and most Smarta Shaiva domestic shrines.

seoFaqs.question: Is the Panchakshara Stotram a substitute for Panchakshara mantra japa?
seoFaqs.answer: No, the two are complementary rather than substitutable. The Panchakshara mantra (Om Namah Shivaya or Namah Shivaya) is a beeja-rich diksha mantra ideally received from a guru and chanted in fixed counts (108, 1008 or longer), while the Panchakshara Stotram is a five-verse hymn that anyone can recite without diksha. Many Shaiva manuals advise reciting the stotram first as a meditative invocation of each syllable's meaning, and then proceeding to mantra japa, so that the chanting is informed by understanding. Devotees without formal initiation often rely on the stotram alone as a complete daily practice, treating it as a sanctioned vehicle for engaging the Panchakshara without violating diksha conventions.

seoFaqs.question: When is the Shiv Panchakshara Stotram traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The stotram is recited daily as part of nitya-puja, especially on Mondays (Shiva-vara), every Pradosham evening (the 13th lunar day twilight), throughout the Shravana and Karthika months, and most importantly on Maha Shivaratri night. In temple settings it is chanted before and after abhishekam to the Shiva lingam. The recommended count varies, one round for daily practice, five or eleven rounds on Mondays and Pradosham, and 108 rounds during Shivaratri parayana. The five-verse length (about 90 seconds per round) makes it well suited to high-count parayana. No fasting or initiation is required, only a clean seat, lit lamp, and basic ritual purity.

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the closing verse of the stotram itself promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti verse appended to the Panchakshara Stotram declares that whoever recites this hymn of the five syllables in the presence of Shiva (i.e., before a Shiva image or lingam) attains the world of Shiva (Shiva-loka) and enjoys Shiva's bliss. Wikipedia, Shlokam and the Greenmesg English translation all reproduce this phala-shruti consistently. Shaiva commentators emphasise that the promise is conditional on ekagrata (one-pointed attention) and bhava (devotional feeling), not on mechanical repetition. Many lineage teachers therefore recommend slow, meaning-aware recitation rather than fast counting, especially during the foundational years of practice, so that the stotram functions as a contemplative pathway into the Panchakshara mantra rather than a numerical accumulation.
